courses.title: Getting Started with Claude Code
courses.description: Install Claude Code, authenticate, and learn the 40-tool architecture that powers the most advanced AI coding assistant.

lessons.title: Installing Claude Code
lessons.description: Install Claude Code via npm or Homebrew and verify your setup.

sections.content:
Installing Claude Code

Claude Code is Anthropic's official CLI for Claude. It runs in your terminal and gives Claude direct access to your codebase, file system, and development tools. Unlike chat-based interfaces, Claude Code operates as an agentic coding partner that can read, write, and execute code autonomously.

Prerequisites

You need Node.js 18+ installed. Check with node --version. You also need an Anthropic API key or a Claude Pro/Max subscription.

Install via npm (recommended)

npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

Install via Homebrew (macOS/Linux)

brew install claude-code

Verify installation

claude --version

This should print the installed version. If you see a command not found error, ensure your global npm bin directory is in your PATH.

Updating

Claude Code updates frequently. Update with:

npm update -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

exercises.question: What is the recommended way to install Claude Code?
exercises.explanation: Claude Code is distributed as an npm package. The global install flag (-g) makes the 'claude' command available system-wide.

exercises.question: What is the minimum Node.js version required for Claude Code?
exercises.explanation: Claude Code requires Node.js 18 or higher. Earlier versions lack required APIs for the streaming and tool execution engine.

exercises.question: How do you update Claude Code to the latest version?
exercises.explanation: Since Claude Code is an npm package, you update it with npm update -g. If you installed via Homebrew, brew upgrade claude-code also works.

lessons.title: First Run: Authentication & Project Setup
lessons.description: Authenticate with Anthropic, initialize your first project, and understand the session lifecycle.

sections.content:
Your First Claude Code Session

Navigate to any project directory and run:

claude

On first run, Claude Code will prompt you to authenticate. You have two options:

Option 1: API Key

Set the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable:

export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...

Add this to your shell profile (~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc) so it persists across sessions.

Option 2: Claude Pro/Max Subscription

Claude Code can authenticate via your Anthropic account. Run claude and follow the browser-based OAuth flow. This links your subscription and you won't need an API key.

Project Initialization

When you run claude in a directory for the first time, it scans the project structure to build context. It reads existing CLAUDE.md files, checks for .claude/ configuration directories, and identifies the project type (language, framework, build tools).

Session Lifecycle

Each claude invocation starts a session. Within a session, Claude maintains context about your conversation, files read, and changes made. When you exit (Ctrl+C or type /exit), the session ends. The next session starts fresh but can leverage persistent memory stored in CLAUDE.md and .claude/ files.

exercises.question: Which environment variable does Claude Code use for API key authentication?
exercises.explanation: Claude Code uses ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, the standard environment variable for all Anthropic API products.

exercises.question: What happens when you run 'claude' in a new project directory for the first time?
options: It creates a new git repository
It generates a full project documentation
It scans the project structure and reads any existing CLAUDE.md files
exercises.explanation: Claude Code scans your project structure on first run, reading CLAUDE.md files and .claude/ configuration to build context about your codebase.

exercises.question: How do you exit a Claude Code session?
exercises.explanation: You can exit a Claude Code session with Ctrl+C or the /exit command. The session context is discarded, but persistent memory in CLAUDE.md survives.

lessons.title: The Tool Permission System
lessons.description: Understand how Claude Code's permission-gated tools work and what Claude can and can't do without your approval.

sections.content:
Permission-Gated Tools

Claude Code doesn't have free rein over your system. Every action it takes goes through a permission system. There are roughly 40 discrete tools, each with its own permission level.

Permission Tiers

Always allowed (no prompt): Reading files, searching code, listing directories. These are read-only operations that can't modify your system.

Requires approval: Writing files, running bash commands, making web requests. Claude will show you exactly what it wants to do and wait for your yes/no. You can also approve tools for the entire session.

Never allowed by default: Certain destructive operations require explicit configuration to enable.

Approving Tools

When Claude requests permission to run a tool, you'll see a prompt like:

Claude wants to run: bash
Command: npm install express
[y/n/always]

Choosing y approves this single invocation. Choosing always approves this tool for the rest of the session. You can also configure allowed tools in your project settings.

The allowedTools Configuration

In your .claude/settings.json or CLAUDE.md, you can pre-approve specific tools or command patterns:

{
  "allowedTools": [
    "bash(npm test)",
    "bash(npm run lint)",
    "edit",
    "read"
  ]
}

This lets Claude run npm test and npm run lint without asking each time, while still requiring approval for other bash commands.

exercises.question: Which of these operations does Claude Code perform WITHOUT asking for permission?
exercises.explanation: Reading files is a read-only operation and is always allowed without prompting. Writing, executing commands, and web requests all require approval.

exercises.question: What does choosing 'always' at a tool permission prompt do?
options: Permanently allows the tool across all projects
Adds the tool to your global allowlist
Allows the tool for the rest of the current session
Allows the tool for the next 5 invocations
exercises.explanation: Choosing 'always' approves that specific tool for the remainder of the current session. It does not persist across sessions.

exercises.question: Write an allowedTools JSON configuration that pre-approves running 'pytest' and 'make build' without prompting, while still requiring approval for other bash commands.
exercises.explanation: Each bash command pattern is specified as bash(command). This allows only those specific commands to run without prompting.

exercises.question: Approximately how many discrete, permission-gated tools does Claude Code have?
exercises.explanation: Claude Code has approximately 40 discrete tools covering file operations, bash execution, web requests, LSP integration, and more.

lessons.title: Basic Commands: Read, Edit, Run
lessons.description: Learn the core workflow of reading files, editing code, and running tests with Claude Code.

sections.content:
The Core Workflow

Claude Code's day-to-day power comes from three tool categories: reading, editing, and executing. Here's how each works under the hood.

Reading Files

Claude uses the Read tool to view file contents. It reads files with line numbers (like cat -n) and can read specific line ranges for large files. Just ask naturally:

"Read the auth middleware in src/middleware/auth.ts"

Claude will use the Read tool, which returns content with line numbers for precise reference. For large files, it can read specific ranges to stay within context limits.

Editing Files

The Edit tool performs surgical string replacements. Claude specifies an exact old_string to find and a new_string to replace it. This is more precise than rewriting entire files and produces clean diffs you can review.

"Add error handling to the login function in auth.ts"

Claude will read the file first, then propose an Edit with the exact change. You'll see the old and new strings before approving.

Running Commands

The Bash tool executes shell commands. Claude uses this for running tests, installing packages, checking git status, and any other terminal operation:

"Run the test suite and fix any failures"

Claude will run your tests, read the output, identify failures, edit the code, and re-run tests, all in a single interaction loop.

The Write Tool

For creating new files or complete rewrites, Claude uses the Write tool. Unlike Edit (which patches), Write replaces the entire file. Claude prefers Edit for modifications to avoid losing surrounding code.

exercises.question: Why does Claude Code prefer the Edit tool over the Write tool for modifying existing files?
options: Edit sends only the diff, avoiding overwriting surrounding code
exercises.explanation: The Edit tool performs targeted string replacements, which is safer because it only changes the specific code being modified. The Write tool overwrites the entire file, risking loss of surrounding content.

exercises.question: What must Claude Code do before using the Edit tool on a file?
options: Read the file first using the Read tool
Create a backup of the file
Lock the file to prevent concurrent edits
exercises.explanation: Claude Code must read a file with the Read tool before editing it. This ensures Claude has accurate, up-to-date content and can specify the correct old_string for replacement.

exercises.question: When Claude runs 'npm test' and finds failures, what is its typical workflow?
options: Report the failures and wait for instructions
Read test output, edit the code to fix failures, re-run tests
Run the tests again hoping they pass
exercises.explanation: Claude Code operates in an agentic loop: run tests, analyze failures, edit code to fix, then re-run to verify. This autonomous fix-verify cycle is one of its most powerful capabilities.

lessons.title: Understanding the 40-Tool Architecture
lessons.description: Deep dive into Claude Code's internal tool system: categories, the Query Engine, and how tools are orchestrated.

sections.content:
The 40-Tool Architecture

Claude Code's power comes from approximately 40 discrete tools organized into categories. Understanding this architecture helps you work with Claude more effectively.

Tool Categories

File Operations: Read, Write, Edit, Glob (file search by pattern), Grep (content search). These handle all file system interactions.

Execution: Bash (shell commands). This is the most versatile tool, handling everything from git operations to running build scripts.

Search & Navigation: Glob uses ripgrep-powered file pattern matching. Grep performs content search with full regex support. Together they let Claude navigate codebases of any size.

Agent Tools: The Agent tool spawns sub-agents for complex tasks. The Skill tool invokes registered skills (custom commands). These enable multi-step orchestration.

Web Tools: WebFetch retrieves content from URLs. WebSearch performs web searches. These connect Claude to external documentation and resources.

MCP Tools: Any tools exposed by connected MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers appear as additional tools. This is how Claude integrates with Supabase, GitHub, Obsidian, and other services.

The Query Engine

Behind all these tools sits the Query Engine, a ~46,000-line system that handles all LLM API calls, streaming, caching, and context management. It decides how to prioritize context when the conversation grows long, compresses less relevant information, and manages the token budget for each request. You don't interact with the Query Engine directly, but understanding that it exists explains why Claude Code stays responsive even in long sessions with large codebases.

Tool Invocation Flow

When Claude decides to use a tool: (1) it generates a tool call with parameters, (2) the permission system checks if approval is needed, (3) you approve or it auto-executes if pre-approved, (4) the tool runs and returns results, (5) Claude processes the results and decides next steps. This loop continues until Claude has enough information to respond or complete the task.

exercises.question: Which tool does Claude Code use for file pattern matching (e.g., finding all .ts files)?
exercises.explanation: Glob is Claude Code's dedicated file pattern matching tool, powered by ripgrep. It's preferred over using bash find commands because it's optimized for the codebase and returns results sorted by modification time.

exercises.question: What is the Query Engine responsible for in Claude Code's architecture?
options: Handling all LLM API calls, streaming, caching, and context management
Managing the permission system for tool approvals
Processing user search queries across files
exercises.explanation: The Query Engine (~46K lines of code) is the backbone that handles all communication with the LLM API, including streaming responses, caching, and managing the context window to keep Claude responsive in long sessions.

exercises.question: Put the tool invocation flow in the correct order:
items: Claude generates a tool call with parameters
Permission system checks if approval is needed
Claude processes results and decides next steps
exercises.explanation: The flow is: Claude generates the call, permissions are checked, user approves (or auto-executes), tool runs, Claude processes results.

exercises.question: How do MCP (Model Context Protocol) tools appear in Claude Code?
options: They appear as additional tools alongside the built-in ones
They replace the built-in tools entirely
They are only available in the web interface
They require a separate CLI command to access
exercises.explanation: MCP tools from connected servers appear as additional tools alongside Claude Code's built-in ~40 tools. This is how integrations with Supabase, GitHub, Obsidian, and other services work.

courses.title: CLAUDE.md & Project Configuration
courses.description: Master the CLAUDE.md file system and project configuration to give Claude persistent context about your codebase.

lessons.title: What CLAUDE.md Does and Where It Lives
lessons.description: Understand the CLAUDE.md file hierarchy and how Claude Code loads project context at session start.

sections.content:
CLAUDE.md: Your Project's Brain

CLAUDE.md is a markdown file that gives Claude persistent context about your project. It's automatically loaded at the start of every session, acting as instructions, memory, and configuration rolled into one.

File Hierarchy

CLAUDE.md files can exist at multiple levels, each with different scope:

Global: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md - Applies to all projects. Put your personal preferences, coding style, and universal rules here.

Project root: ./CLAUDE.md - Applies to the current project. This is the most common location. Put project-specific stack info, conventions, and architecture notes here.

Subdirectory: ./src/CLAUDE.md - Applies when Claude is working in that directory. Use for module-specific context.

Loading Order

Claude loads these files in order from global to local. Later files can override earlier ones. All content is injected into the system prompt, so Claude sees it as authoritative instructions.

What Makes a Good CLAUDE.md

Think of CLAUDE.md as onboarding documentation for an extremely capable but context-free developer joining your project. Include: tech stack, key commands (build, test, lint), project structure, coding conventions, and any domain-specific terminology.

exercises.question: Where should you put CLAUDE.md settings that apply to ALL your projects?
exercises.explanation: The global CLAUDE.md lives at ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. It's loaded for every Claude Code session regardless of which project directory you're in.

exercises.question: When both a global and project CLAUDE.md exist, which takes precedence?
options: Only one is loaded, chosen at random
The project one, since it loads later and can override
exercises.explanation: CLAUDE.md files load from global to local. The project-level file loads after the global one, so its instructions can override or refine global settings.

exercises.question: What is the best analogy for what CLAUDE.md does?
options: Onboarding documentation for a new developer
exercises.explanation: CLAUDE.md is like onboarding docs for a skilled but context-free developer. It provides the project knowledge, conventions, and commands that Claude needs to work effectively in your codebase.

lessons.title: Writing Effective CLAUDE.md Files
lessons.description: Learn the patterns and anti-patterns for writing CLAUDE.md files that maximize Claude's effectiveness.

sections.content:
Writing Effective CLAUDE.md Files

A well-written CLAUDE.md dramatically improves Claude's output quality. Here are the patterns that work.

Essential Sections

# Project Name

Brief description of what this project does.

## Tech Stack
- Runtime: Node.js 20, TypeScript 5.4
- Framework: Next.js 14 (App Router)
- Database: PostgreSQL via Supabase
- Styling: Tailwind CSS v4

## Key Commands
- `npm run dev` - Start dev server
- `npm test` - Run test suite
- `npm run lint` - Lint and format
- `npm run build` - Production build

## Project Structure
src/
  app/       # Next.js App Router pages
  lib/       # Shared utilities
  components/ # React components

## Conventions
- Use named exports, not default exports
- All components are functional with hooks
- Database queries go in src/lib/db/
- Error handling: throw, don't return error codes

Anti-Patterns to Avoid

Too vague: "This is a web app" tells Claude nothing useful. Be specific about frameworks and versions.

Too verbose: Don't paste entire documentation. CLAUDE.md content consumes context window tokens. Keep it concise and high-signal.

Contradictory instructions: If you say "always use semicolons" in global CLAUDE.md but "never use semicolons" in a project file, Claude will follow the project file but may be confused. Be intentional about overrides.

Power Patterns

Rules as imperatives: Write rules as direct commands. "Always run tests before committing" is clearer than "It would be nice if tests were run before commits."

Examples over descriptions: Show a code example of your preferred pattern rather than describing it in prose.

exercises.question: Write a CLAUDE.md Tech Stack section for a Python FastAPI project that uses PostgreSQL, Redis for caching, and pytest for testing.
exercises.correct_answer:
## Tech Stack
- Runtime: Python 3.12
- Framework: FastAPI
- Database: PostgreSQL
- Caching: Redis
- Testing: pytest
exercises.explanation: List each technology with its role. Be specific about versions when they matter. This gives Claude the context to generate compatible code.

exercises.question: Write a CLAUDE.md Key Commands section for a project that uses 'make test', 'make lint', 'make build', and 'docker-compose up' for local dev.
exercises.correct_answer:
## Key Commands
- `make test` - Run test suite
- `make lint` - Lint and format
- `make build` - Production build
- `docker-compose up` - Start local dev environment
exercises.explanation: Key Commands tells Claude exactly how to build, test, and run your project. Without this, Claude has to guess or search for a Makefile.

exercises.question: Why should you avoid pasting entire documentation into CLAUDE.md?
options: It wastes context window tokens with low-signal content
exercises.explanation: CLAUDE.md content is injected into the system prompt, consuming context window tokens. Long, low-signal content pushes out space for actual code and conversation. Keep it concise and high-value.

exercises.question: Which is a more effective CLAUDE.md rule?
options: "Always write unit tests for new functions before committing"
"It would be nice to have tests for new functions"
exercises.explanation: Imperative commands are clearest. 'Always write unit tests for new functions before committing' is an unambiguous directive that Claude will follow consistently.

lessons.title: Path-Specific Rules with .claude/rules/
lessons.description: Use the .claude/rules/ directory to apply different instructions to different parts of your codebase.

sections.content:
Path-Specific Rules

While CLAUDE.md provides project-wide instructions, the .claude/rules/ directory lets you apply different rules to different parts of your codebase.

How It Works

Create markdown files in .claude/rules/. Each file can specify which paths it applies to using frontmatter:

---
globs: ["src/api/**/*.ts", "src/routes/**/*.ts"]
---

# API Rules

- All endpoints must have input validation using zod schemas
- Always return proper HTTP status codes
- Include rate limiting middleware on public endpoints
- Never expose internal error messages to clients

Use Cases

Security rules for API code: Validation, sanitization, auth checks.

Style rules for frontend: Component patterns, accessibility requirements.

Database rules: Always use parameterized queries, include RLS policies.

Test rules: Naming conventions, assertion patterns, mocking guidelines.

Global Rules

You can also place rules files in ~/.claude/rules/ for personal rules that apply across all your projects. For example, a security.md file with your security checklist that applies everywhere.

Rule File Naming

Name your rule files descriptively: security.md, frontend.md, database.md, testing.md. The filename helps you organize but doesn't affect how rules are applied. Only the globs in the frontmatter control scoping.

exercises.question: Write a .claude/rules/testing.md file with frontmatter that applies to all test files (*.test.ts and *.spec.ts) and includes rules for using describe/it blocks and avoiding snapshot tests.
exercises.correct_answer:
---
globs: ["**/*.test.ts", "**/*.spec.ts"]
---

# Testing Rules

- Use describe/it blocks for all tests
- Avoid snapshot tests; use explicit assertions
- Each test should test one behavior
- Use descriptive test names that explain the expected behavior
exercises.explanation: The globs frontmatter targets the rules to specific file patterns. These rules will only be injected when Claude is working with matching test files.

exercises.question: What controls which files a rule in .claude/rules/ applies to?
options: The globs field in the file's frontmatter
The filename of the rule file
A separate config file mapping rules to paths
The directory the rule file is in
exercises.explanation: The globs field in the frontmatter (YAML at the top of the file between --- markers) specifies which file paths the rules apply to.

exercises.question: Where would you put a personal security checklist that should apply to all your projects?
exercises.explanation: Personal rules that apply globally go in ~/.claude/rules/. These are loaded for every project, so your security checklist will always be active.

lessons.title: Memory System: How Memories Work
lessons.description: Learn the four types of Claude Code memories and how they persist knowledge across sessions.

sections.content:
The Memory System

Claude Code's memory system lets knowledge persist across sessions. Unlike CLAUDE.md (which you write manually), memories are created during conversations and stored for future reference.

Memory Types

There are four categories of memories, each serving a different purpose:

User memories (prefix: user_): Personal preferences about you. Example: "User prefers TypeScript over JavaScript" or "User wants confirmation before any git push." These persist globally.

Feedback memories (prefix: feedback_): Corrections and refinements you've made. When you tell Claude "don't use em dashes" or "always use named exports," it saves this as feedback memory. These teach Claude your style over time.

Project memories (prefix: project_): Facts about specific projects. Architecture decisions, deployment targets, API integrations. These are scoped to the project directory.

Reference memories (prefix: reference_): Technical reference material. Database schemas, API documentation summaries, configuration details.

How Memories Are Created

Memories are created when Claude learns something important during a conversation. You can also explicitly ask: "Remember that we deploy to Vercel, not Netlify." Claude saves a memory file in the appropriate location.

Where Memories Live

Memories are stored as markdown files in:

  • ~/.claude/ for global memories (user and feedback types)
  • .claude/ in the project directory for project and reference types

exercises.question: Which memory type would store the fact that 'this project deploys to Render, not Vercel'?
exercises.explanation: Project-specific facts like deployment targets are stored as project_ memories. They're scoped to the project directory and loaded when you work in that project.

exercises.question: If you tell Claude 'never use default exports in my code,' what type of memory is created?
exercises.explanation: Style corrections and preferences you communicate during a session are saved as feedback_ memories. These teach Claude your coding style over time.

exercises.question: Where are project-scoped memories stored on disk?
exercises.explanation: Project-scoped memories (project_ and reference_ types) are stored in the .claude/ directory within the project. Global memories (user_ and feedback_) live in ~/.claude/.

lessons.title: MEMORY.md Index & Memory File Structure
lessons.description: Learn how MEMORY.md serves as an index to detailed memory files and how to structure them with frontmatter.

sections.content:
MEMORY.md as an Index

As your project accumulates memories, individual files can become hard to manage. MEMORY.md acts as a central index that links to detailed topic files.

Index Structure

# Project Memory

## Voice Agent
- [project_voice_agent.md](memory/project_voice_agent.md) - Architecture and config
- Sloane: sales voice agent, phone + web
- Sarah: PI intake demo, Cartesia voice

## Deployment
- Hosting: Vercel (auto-deploys from main)
- Domain: myapp.com

The index has brief one-line entries. Details go in individual memory files linked from the index.

Memory File Frontmatter

Individual memory files use YAML frontmatter to indicate their type and scope:

---
type: project
created: 2026-03-15
tags: [deployment, infrastructure]
---

# Deployment Configuration

This project deploys to Vercel from the main branch.
The production URL is myapp.com.
Staging deploys from the staging branch to staging.myapp.com.

Best Practices

Keep the index lean: MEMORY.md should stay under 200 lines. One line per topic with a link to the detailed file.

Use topic files for depth: Architecture details, API documentation, complex configurations all belong in individual memory files, not the index.

Maintain the index: After significant changes, ask Claude to update MEMORY.md. You can also use the /revise-claude-md skill to audit and improve your memory files.

exercises.question: Write a MEMORY.md index entry for a project that has a payments integration using Stripe, with details in a separate file.
exercises.correct_answer:
## Payments
- [project_payments.md](memory/project_payments.md) - Stripe integration details
- Stripe Checkout for one-time payments, Stripe Billing for subscriptions
- Webhook endpoint: /api/webhooks/stripe
exercises.explanation: The index entry has a link to the detailed file plus a couple of key facts. This gives Claude quick context without needing to read the full file unless it needs payment-specific details.

exercises.question: Write the frontmatter for a reference memory file about your project's Supabase database schema.
exercises.correct_answer:
---
type: reference
created: 2026-03-30
tags: [database, supabase, schema]
---
exercises.explanation: Reference memories use type: reference and include relevant tags for discoverability. The created date helps you track when the information was captured.

exercises.question: What is the recommended maximum line count for MEMORY.md?
exercises.explanation: MEMORY.md should stay under 200 lines. Beyond that, it consumes too much context window and becomes hard to maintain. Move details into individual topic files and keep the index lean.

courses.title: Plan Mode & Task Decomposition
courses.description: Learn how Claude Code decides between planning and executing, and how to guide it for complex multi-file changes.

lessons.title: When Claude Enters Plan Mode vs Direct Execution
lessons.description: Understand Claude's decision-making process for when to plan and when to act immediately.

sections.content:
Plan Mode vs Direct Execution

Claude Code dynamically decides whether to plan first or execute directly based on task complexity. Understanding this decision process helps you work with Claude more effectively.

Direct Execution (Simple Tasks)

For straightforward requests, Claude acts immediately:

  • "Fix the typo on line 42"
  • "Add a console.log to the login function"
  • "Run the tests"
  • "What does the auth middleware do?"

These are single-step operations with clear outcomes. Planning would just add overhead.

Plan Mode (Complex Tasks)

For multi-step or ambiguous requests, Claude plans before acting:

  • "Refactor the auth system to use JWT tokens"
  • "Add a new user profile page with form validation"
  • "Migrate from REST to GraphQL"

In plan mode, Claude outlines the steps it will take, the files it will modify, and the order of operations before touching any code.

How Claude Decides

Claude evaluates: (1) how many files are involved, (2) whether the task has dependencies between steps, (3) how ambiguous the request is, and (4) the potential for unintended side effects. More files, more dependencies, and more ambiguity push toward planning.

exercises.question: Which task would most likely trigger plan mode?
options: Fix a typo in the README
Add rate limiting to all API endpoints
exercises.explanation: Adding rate limiting to all API endpoints involves multiple files, middleware configuration, and potentially database changes. This complexity triggers plan mode. The other tasks are single-step operations.

exercises.question: What factors push Claude toward using plan mode?
options: The number of previous sessions on the project
Multiple files, step dependencies, and ambiguous requirements
Large file sizes and slow network
exercises.explanation: Claude evaluates file count, inter-step dependencies, and request ambiguity. More of these factors means more planning is needed to avoid mistakes.

exercises.question: Why does Claude skip planning for simple tasks?
options: The API doesn't support planning for simple requests
Planning is disabled for small files
Claude can't plan for tasks under 10 lines of code
Planning adds overhead that isn't needed for single-step operations
exercises.explanation: For a single-step task like fixing a typo, creating a plan would waste time and tokens. Claude executes directly because the cost-benefit doesn't justify planning.

lessons.title: How to Trigger Plan Mode
lessons.description: Learn commands and patterns to explicitly control when Claude plans before executing.

sections.content:
Controlling Plan Mode

While Claude auto-detects when planning is needed, you can override this behavior.

Explicitly Triggering Plan Mode

Add "plan first" or "make a plan" to your request:

"Plan first, then add authentication to the API"
"I need you to make a plan for migrating our database schema"

This forces Claude to produce a structured plan before writing any code, even if it would have executed directly.

When to Force Planning

Force planning when:

  • You want to review the approach before code changes happen
  • The task involves critical systems (auth, payments, data migrations)
  • You're unsure about the right approach and want options
  • Multiple valid implementations exist and you want to choose

Skipping Plan Mode

For tasks where Claude is over-planning, be direct:

"Just fix the import statement in app.ts, don't plan"

Or provide such specific instructions that planning becomes unnecessary:

"In src/auth.ts, line 45, change the bcrypt rounds from 10 to 12"

Using the Plan Command

You can also use the /plan command or the plan skill to create a formal, structured plan that can be saved and executed later, potentially by sub-agents.

exercises.question: How do you explicitly force Claude to plan before executing?
exercises.explanation: Adding 'plan first' or 'make a plan' to your request explicitly tells Claude to produce a structured plan before writing any code.

exercises.question: When should you force plan mode even if Claude would execute directly?
options: When changing critical systems like auth or payments
exercises.explanation: Critical systems like authentication and payments deserve a reviewed plan before any code changes. The cost of a mistake is too high for direct execution.

exercises.question: Write a prompt that forces Claude to plan a database migration from MySQL to PostgreSQL, showing you the steps before executing anything.
exercises.correct_answer: Plan first. I need to migrate our database from MySQL to PostgreSQL. Show me the full plan with all files that will change, the migration order, and any breaking changes before you touch any code.
exercises.explanation: Starting with 'Plan first' triggers plan mode. Specifying what you want in the plan (files, order, breaking changes) ensures Claude produces a comprehensive plan you can review.

lessons.title: Writing Good Task Descriptions
lessons.description: Learn how to describe tasks to Claude Code for optimal results: the right level of specificity and context.

sections.content:
Writing Good Task Descriptions

The quality of Claude's output is directly proportional to the quality of your input. Here's how to describe tasks effectively.

The Goal-Oriented Approach

Pass goals, not step-by-step instructions. Claude is better at figuring out the implementation when you tell it what you want, not how to build it.

Bad: "Open src/auth.ts, find the login function, add a try-catch block around the database query, then add a new error type in src/types.ts, then update the error handler in src/middleware/errors.ts"

Good: "The login endpoint returns a 500 when the database is down. Add proper error handling so it returns a user-friendly 503 with a retry message."

The good version describes the problem and desired outcome. Claude figures out which files to modify and what pattern to use.

Provide Context, Not Instructions

  • What the current behavior is
  • What the desired behavior is
  • Any constraints (performance, backwards compatibility, specific libraries)

Specificity Sweet Spot

Too vague: "Make the app faster." (What part? Faster how?)

Too specific: "Change line 42 of file X to Y." (Why not just do it yourself?)

Just right: "The dashboard loads slowly because we're fetching all users. Add pagination to the /api/users endpoint, 50 per page."

exercises.question: Why is 'pass goals, not step-by-step instructions' better for Claude Code?
options: Claude is better at choosing the right implementation when it understands the goal
exercises.explanation: Claude has deep knowledge of patterns and best practices. When you describe the goal, Claude can choose the optimal implementation approach. Step-by-step instructions can lead it down suboptimal paths.

exercises.question: Rewrite this overly vague task into a well-scoped request: 'Fix the form'
exercises.correct_answer: The signup form on /register doesn't show validation errors when the email is invalid. Add client-side email validation that shows an inline error message below the email field.
exercises.explanation: A good task description includes: which form, what's broken, what the fix should look like. This gives Claude everything it needs without dictating implementation details.

exercises.question: What three pieces of context should a good task description include?
options: Current behavior, desired behavior, and constraints
File names, line numbers, and exact code changes
Programming language, framework version, and OS
exercises.explanation: Current behavior (what's happening), desired behavior (what should happen), and constraints (performance, compatibility, etc.) give Claude the full picture to plan an effective solution.

lessons.title: Multi-File Changes & Codebase Navigation
lessons.description: How Claude navigates large codebases and coordinates changes across multiple files.

sections.content:
Multi-File Changes

Real features touch multiple files: a new API endpoint needs a route, controller, model, tests, and possibly frontend changes. Here's how Claude handles this.

Codebase Navigation

Claude uses a search-first approach to understand your codebase:

  1. Glob to find relevant files by pattern ("find all route files")
  2. Grep to search for specific code patterns ("where is the User model imported?")
  3. Read to examine specific files in detail

This is why CLAUDE.md with a project structure section is so valuable. It gives Claude a map instead of requiring exploratory searching.

Change Coordination

For multi-file changes, Claude follows a pattern:

  1. Identify all files that need to change
  2. Understand the dependencies between changes (which must happen first)
  3. Make changes in dependency order
  4. Run tests to verify nothing broke

Handling Large Files

Claude's Read tool can read specific line ranges, not just entire files. For large files (1000+ lines), Claude reads targeted sections rather than loading everything. This keeps the context window focused on relevant code.

Tips for Multi-File Tasks

  • Mention the general area of the codebase if you know it
  • Reference existing patterns: "follow the same pattern as the /users endpoint"
  • Ask Claude to run tests after each major change, not just at the end

exercises.question: Put Claude's multi-file change workflow in the correct order:
items: Identify all files that need to change
exercises.explanation: Claude first identifies affected files, then maps dependencies, makes changes in the right order, and finally runs tests to verify the result.

exercises.question: Why does Claude read specific line ranges instead of entire large files?
options: The Read tool has a 100-line limit
Large files cause syntax errors in the tool
Large files take too long to download
It keeps the context window focused on relevant code
exercises.explanation: The context window has a token limit. Reading entire large files wastes tokens on irrelevant code. Targeted reads keep the context focused and leave room for other file contents and conversation.

exercises.question: What's the most effective way to help Claude navigate a new codebase?
options: Paste all file contents into the chat
Create an index.ts that re-exports everything
Include a project structure section in CLAUDE.md
exercises.explanation: A project structure section in CLAUDE.md gives Claude a map of your codebase. It can then navigate efficiently with targeted searches instead of exploratory scanning.

lessons.title: The Query Engine: Context Prioritization
lessons.description: Understand how Claude Code's Query Engine manages context, compresses information, and prioritizes what to keep in the conversation.

sections.content:
The Query Engine

Claude Code's Query Engine is the ~46,000-line system responsible for all communication with the LLM API. Understanding its behavior helps you work within its strengths.

Context Window Management

Every piece of information in a Claude Code session competes for space in the context window: your conversation history, file contents Claude has read, tool results, CLAUDE.md content, and system instructions. The Query Engine manages this budget.

How Context is Prioritized

When the context window fills up, the Query Engine makes decisions about what to keep and what to compress:

  1. System instructions (CLAUDE.md, rules): Always kept at full fidelity
  2. Recent conversation: The most recent messages are kept intact
  3. Recent tool results: Recent file reads and command outputs are preserved
  4. Older conversation: Earlier messages may be summarized or truncated
  5. Old tool results: File contents read many turns ago may be dropped

Practical Implications

Re-read files before editing: If you read a file 20 messages ago, ask Claude to re-read it before making changes. The original content may have been compressed.

Keep sessions focused: A session that starts with feature A and pivots to feature B accumulates stale context from A. Starting a fresh session for unrelated work gives Claude a cleaner context.

Use CLAUDE.md for persistent context: Information in CLAUDE.md is always present. If you find yourself repeating the same context each session, put it in CLAUDE.md.

Caching

The Query Engine also implements caching to reduce API calls and speed up responses. Repeated file reads within a session may be served from cache rather than re-reading the filesystem.

exercises.question: What type of content has the HIGHEST priority in the Query Engine's context management?
exercises.explanation: System instructions from CLAUDE.md and rules files are always kept at full fidelity. They define the project context and Claude's behavior, so they're never compressed or dropped.

exercises.question: Why should you start a fresh session when switching between unrelated tasks?
options: The API key expires after one task
Stale context from the previous task wastes context window space
Each task requires a separate CLAUDE.md
exercises.explanation: Continuing a session accumulates context from the previous task. This old context wastes tokens and can confuse Claude. A fresh session gives a clean context window for the new work.

exercises.question: If you read a file 20 messages ago and now want to edit it, what should you do?
options: Copy the file contents into the chat
Ask Claude to re-read the file before editing
Just tell Claude to edit it; it remembers everything
exercises.explanation: File contents from many turns ago may have been compressed or dropped by the Query Engine. Re-reading ensures Claude has the current, accurate file content before making changes.

courses.title: Custom Commands & Skills
courses.description: Create reusable slash commands and skills that extend Claude Code's capabilities for your specific workflows.

lessons.title: Creating Slash Commands
lessons.description: Build custom slash commands that encapsulate common workflows into single-command shortcuts.

sections.content:
Custom Slash Commands

Claude Code supports custom slash commands that let you create shortcuts for common workflows. Instead of typing the same instructions repeatedly, define them once and invoke with a single command.

Command File Location

Custom commands are markdown files stored in .claude/commands/ within your project:

.claude/
  commands/
    deploy.md
    review.md
    test-and-fix.md

Each file becomes a slash command named after the file. The above creates /deploy, /review, and /test-and-fix.

Command File Structure

A command file is simply a markdown file containing the prompt Claude should execute:

# /deploy

Run the full deployment checklist:
1. Run `npm test` and ensure all tests pass
2. Run `npm run lint` and fix any issues
3. Run `npm run build` and verify no errors
4. Check git status for uncommitted changes
5. If all checks pass, create a commit with a descriptive message
6. Push to the main branch

Using Arguments

Commands can accept arguments using the $ARGUMENTS placeholder:

# /fix-issue

Look at GitHub issue $ARGUMENTS.
Read the issue description, understand the bug, and implement a fix.
Run tests to verify the fix works.
Create a commit referencing the issue number.

Usage: /fix-issue #42

Personal vs Project Commands

Commands in .claude/commands/ are project-specific and can be committed to git for the team. Commands in ~/.claude/commands/ are personal and available across all projects.

exercises.question: Create a slash command file for /pr that creates a pull request: runs tests, checks lint, creates a commit, pushes, and opens a PR with a generated title and description. The command should accept a branch name as an argument.
exercises.correct_answer:
# /pr

Create a pull request for the current changes:
1. Run the test suite and fix any failures
2. Run the linter and fix any issues
3. Create a commit with a descriptive message
4. Push to branch $ARGUMENTS (or create a new branch if not specified)
5. Open a PR using `gh pr create` with a generated title and summary
6. Return the PR URL
exercises.explanation: The $ARGUMENTS placeholder captures whatever follows the /pr command. The steps are goal-oriented, letting Claude figure out the specific commands for your project.

exercises.question: Where should you put a slash command that should be available to your entire team?
exercises.explanation: Commands in .claude/commands/ are project-specific and can be committed to git, making them available to the entire team. Personal commands in ~/.claude/commands/ are only available to you.

exercises.question: How do you pass arguments to a custom slash command?
options: Arguments are defined in a config file
The command uses $ARGUMENTS to capture everything after the command name
Pass a JSON object after the command
exercises.explanation: The $ARGUMENTS placeholder in the command file captures all text after the command name. For example, '/fix-issue #42' passes '#42' as the arguments.

lessons.title: Building Custom Skills (SKILL.md)
lessons.description: Create full-featured skills with SKILL.md files that define triggers, prompts, and specialized behaviors.

sections.content:
Building Custom Skills

Skills are more powerful than slash commands. They include trigger conditions, detailed prompts, and can be automatically activated when Claude detects a matching context.

Skill File Structure

Skills live in .claude/skills/ and use a SKILL.md format:

.claude/
  skills/
    code-review/
      SKILL.md
    db-migration/
      SKILL.md

SKILL.md Anatomy

---
name: code-review
description: "Review code changes for security, performance, and correctness"
trigger: "Use when the user asks to review code or says 'review this'"
---

# Code Review Skill

When triggered, perform a structured code review:

## Steps
1. Run `git diff` to see all changes
2. For each changed file, check:
   - Security: SQL injection, XSS, auth bypass, exposed secrets
   - Performance: N+1 queries, unnecessary re-renders, missing indexes
   - Correctness: Edge cases, error handling, type safety
3. Output findings organized by severity (Critical > High > Medium > Low)
4. Suggest specific fixes for Critical and High issues

## Output Format
Use markdown with severity badges and code snippets showing the issue and fix.

Key Components

name: How the skill is referenced internally and in logs.

description: Short summary shown when listing available skills.

trigger: Natural language description of when this skill should activate. Claude matches incoming requests against these triggers.

Body: The detailed prompt that Claude follows when the skill is invoked.

exercises.question: Write the frontmatter for a SKILL.md that creates a database migration skill. It should trigger when the user mentions 'migration', 'migrate', or 'schema change'.
exercises.correct_answer:
---
name: db-migration
description: "Generate and run database migrations for schema changes"
trigger: "Use when the user mentions migration, migrate, schema change, or altering database tables"
---
exercises.explanation: The trigger field uses natural language to describe when Claude should activate this skill. It matches against the user's request, not exact keywords.

exercises.question: What is the key difference between a slash command and a skill?
options: Skills have trigger conditions and can activate automatically
Skills require an API key but slash commands don't
Slash commands can accept arguments but skills cannot
exercises.explanation: Skills include trigger conditions that let them activate automatically when Claude detects a matching context. Slash commands must be explicitly invoked with /command-name.

exercises.question: Where do skill files live in the project structure?
exercises.explanation: Each skill gets its own directory under .claude/skills/ with a SKILL.md file inside. This allows skills to include additional files alongside the main skill definition.

lessons.title: Skill Triggers and Activation
lessons.description: Understand how Claude matches user requests to skills and the priority system for trigger resolution.

sections.content:
Skill Triggers and Activation

Skills can be invoked in two ways: explicitly (using the Skill tool or a slash command reference) or automatically (when Claude's request matches a trigger).

Explicit Invocation

You can directly ask for a skill:

"Use the code-review skill on these changes"

Or reference it as a slash command if named appropriately:

"/code-review"

Automatic Trigger Matching

When you make a request, Claude checks it against all registered skill triggers. The trigger field in SKILL.md is a natural language description, not a regex or keyword list. Claude semantically matches your request against available triggers.

For example, a skill with trigger "Use when reviewing pull requests or checking code quality" would match:

  • "Can you review this PR?"
  • "Check the quality of my changes"
  • "Look over this code before I merge"

Priority Resolution

When multiple skills could match, Claude considers:

  1. Specificity: More specific triggers win over general ones
  2. Relevance: How closely the request matches the trigger description
  3. Recency: Skills used recently in the session may have slight priority

Writing Good Triggers

Be specific about WHEN to use the skill, not just WHAT it does:

Good: "Use when the user asks to review code, check a PR, or says 'review this' before merging"

Bad: "Code review skill" (too vague, will match too many requests)

exercises.question: How does Claude match user requests to skill triggers?
options: Semantic matching against natural language descriptions
exercises.explanation: Skill triggers are natural language descriptions, and Claude semantically matches incoming requests against them. This means the trigger 'review code' can match 'check my PR' or 'look over these changes'.

exercises.question: Write a trigger description for a skill that should activate when users want to set up a new API endpoint, including routes, controllers, and tests.
exercises.correct_answer: trigger: "Use when the user wants to create a new API endpoint, add a route, build a REST resource, or scaffold an endpoint with controller and tests"
exercises.explanation: Good triggers list multiple phrasings and include related concepts (route, endpoint, REST resource, scaffold). This increases the chance of matching the user's natural language.

exercises.question: When two skills could match a request, what is the PRIMARY factor Claude uses to decide?
exercises.explanation: More specific trigger matches take priority. A skill that specifically mentions 'database migration' will beat a generic 'database operations' skill when the user asks about migrations.

lessons.title: Sharing Skills Across Projects
lessons.description: Package, distribute, and manage skills across your team and projects.

sections.content:
Sharing Skills Across Projects

Skills become more valuable when shared. Here's how to make your skills portable and distribute them.

Personal Skills (Global)

Skills in ~/.claude/skills/ are available in all your projects. Great for personal workflows like your commit style, review process, or debugging approach.

Project Skills (Team)

Skills in .claude/skills/ (committed to git) are available to everyone who clones the repo. Use these for team conventions, project-specific workflows, and shared processes.

Skill Portability

Since skills are just markdown files, sharing is straightforward:

  • Copy the skill directory to another project's .claude/skills/
  • Create a shared git repo of skills and symlink them
  • Use a script to sync skills across multiple projects

Parameterizing Skills for Reuse

Make skills reusable by keeping them framework-agnostic where possible:

---
name: security-audit
description: "Run a security audit on the codebase"
trigger: "Use when the user asks for a security review or audit"
---

# Security Audit

1. Check CLAUDE.md for the project's tech stack
2. Based on the stack, check for:
   - If Node.js: npm audit, check for eval(), unsanitized inputs
   - If Python: check for shell injection, SQL injection, pickle usage
   - For all: hardcoded secrets, exposed API keys, missing auth
3. Report findings by severity

This skill adapts to any project by reading the stack from CLAUDE.md rather than hardcoding assumptions.

exercises.question: How do you make a skill available to your entire team?
options: Share it via a Slack message
Put it in ~/.claude/skills/ on each team member's machine
Put it in .claude/skills/ and commit it to git
Upload it to the Anthropic skill registry
exercises.explanation: Skills in .claude/skills/ are part of the project and can be committed to git. Anyone who clones the repo gets the skills automatically.

exercises.question: What makes a skill more portable across different projects?
options: Reading the project's tech stack from CLAUDE.md instead of hardcoding assumptions
Keeping the skill file under 50 lines
Writing it in TypeScript instead of markdown
exercises.explanation: A portable skill adapts to its context by reading project information from CLAUDE.md rather than hardcoding framework-specific assumptions. This lets the same skill work across Python, Node.js, and other projects.

exercises.question: Where do personal skills that should work across all your projects go?
exercises.explanation: Personal global skills go in ~/.claude/skills/. They're loaded for every Claude Code session regardless of which project you're working in.

lessons.title: Exercise: Write a Custom Skill
lessons.description: Hands-on exercise: build a complete custom skill from scratch with triggers, prompts, and error handling.

sections.content:
Exercise: Build a Deployment Readiness Skill

In this exercise, you'll build a complete skill that checks if a project is ready for deployment. This is a practical skill that many teams need.

Requirements

Your skill should:

  1. Have proper frontmatter with name, description, and trigger
  2. Run the test suite and verify all tests pass
  3. Check for linting errors
  4. Verify no TODO or FIXME comments in production code
  5. Check for hardcoded secrets or API keys
  6. Verify the build succeeds
  7. Check git status for uncommitted changes
  8. Produce a pass/fail report with details for each check

Skill Design Tips

  • Use numbered steps that Claude can follow sequentially
  • Specify what commands to run (but let Claude adapt to the project's actual setup)
  • Define the output format so results are consistent
  • Include error handling: what should happen if a check fails?

Testing Your Skill

After writing the skill, you can test it by:

  1. Creating the file at .claude/skills/deploy-ready/SKILL.md
  2. Starting a new Claude Code session
  3. Typing /deploy-ready or asking "Is this project ready to deploy?"

exercises.question: Write the complete SKILL.md file for a deploy-ready skill that performs the checks listed in the exercise requirements.
exercises.correct_answer:
---
name: deploy-ready
description: "Check if the project is ready for deployment with comprehensive pre-deploy checks"
trigger: "Use when the user asks if the project is ready to deploy, wants a pre-deployment check, or says 'deploy ready'"
---

# Deployment Readiness Check

Perform each check sequentially. Stop and report if any Critical check fails.

## Checks

1. **Tests** (Critical): Run the project's test suite. All tests must pass.
2. **Lint** (Critical): Run the linter. No errors allowed (warnings are acceptable).
3. **Secrets scan** (Critical): Grep for hardcoded API keys, passwords, tokens, and secrets in source files. Exclude .env.example files.
4. **TODO/FIXME** (Warning): Search for TODO and FIXME comments in src/ and app/ directories. Report count but don't fail.
5. **Build** (Critical): Run the production build. Must complete without errors.
6. **Git status** (Warning): Check for uncommitted changes. Report but don't fail.

## Output Format

Produce a deployment readiness report:
- List each check with PASS/FAIL/WARN status
- For failed checks, include the specific error output
- End with a summary: READY TO DEPLOY or NOT READY with reasons
exercises.explanation: The skill has clear frontmatter, severity levels for each check (Critical vs Warning), sequential steps, and a defined output format. It's framework-agnostic since it uses the project's own test/lint/build commands.

exercises.question: Where should you save this skill file to make it available in your project?
exercises.explanation: Skills go in .claude/skills/{skill-name}/SKILL.md. Each skill gets its own directory, allowing it to include additional files alongside the main definition.

exercises.question: Why does the skill distinguish between Critical and Warning checks?
options: Warnings are only checked on weekdays
It's required by the SKILL.md format
Critical failures should block deployment while warnings are informational
Warnings are logged but not displayed
exercises.explanation: Not all issues are deployment blockers. Critical checks (tests, build, secrets) must pass. Warning checks (TODOs, uncommitted changes) are worth knowing about but shouldn't prevent deployment.

courses.title: MCP Servers
courses.description: Connect Claude Code to external services through the Model Context Protocol, extending its capabilities with databases, APIs, and custom tools.

lessons.title: What MCP (Model Context Protocol) Is
lessons.description: Understand the Model Context Protocol and how it extends Claude Code's tool system with external integrations.

sections.content:
What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard created by Anthropic that lets AI models connect to external tools and data sources through a standardized interface. Think of it as USB for AI: a universal connector that lets Claude plug into any service.

Why MCP Matters

Without MCP, each integration requires custom code. Want Claude to query your database? Write a custom tool. Want it to manage GitHub issues? Another custom tool. MCP standardizes this so any MCP-compatible server can be plugged into any MCP-compatible client (like Claude Code).

How It Works

An MCP server is a lightweight program that exposes tools, resources, and prompts via the MCP protocol. Claude Code acts as an MCP client that connects to these servers.

When connected, the server's tools appear alongside Claude Code's built-in tools. Claude can call them the same way it calls Read, Edit, or Bash.

What MCP Servers Provide

  • Tools: Functions Claude can call (query database, create issue, send message)
  • Resources: Data Claude can read (database schemas, API docs, file contents)
  • Prompts: Pre-built prompt templates for common tasks

The Ecosystem

There's a growing ecosystem of MCP servers for popular services: Supabase, GitHub, Slack, Obsidian, PostgreSQL, and many more. You can also build your own MCP servers for internal tools.

exercises.question: What is the best analogy for MCP?
options: USB for AI: a universal connector for external services
exercises.explanation: MCP is like USB: a standardized protocol that lets any compatible tool connect to any compatible AI client. Just as USB eliminated proprietary connectors, MCP eliminates custom integration code.

exercises.question: What three things can an MCP server expose?
exercises.explanation: MCP servers can expose tools (callable functions), resources (readable data), and prompts (pre-built templates). Tools are the most commonly used capability.

exercises.question: Who created the MCP standard?
exercises.explanation: Anthropic created and open-sourced the Model Context Protocol. It's designed to be an industry standard that any AI provider or tool builder can adopt.

lessons.title: Connecting to MCP Servers
lessons.description: Configure .mcp.json to connect Claude Code to MCP servers and manage server lifecycle.

sections.content:
Connecting to MCP Servers

MCP servers are configured in a .mcp.json file. This file tells Claude Code which servers to connect to and how to start them.

Configuration File Locations

Project-level: .mcp.json in your project root. Servers here are only available when working in this project.

Global: ~/.mcp.json for servers you want available across all projects.

Configuration Format

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "supabase": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@supabase/mcp-server"],
      "env": {
        "SUPABASE_URL": "https://your-project.supabase.co",
        "SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY": "your-key"
      }
    },
    "github": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"],
      "env": {
        "GITHUB_TOKEN": "ghp_your-token"
      }
    }
  }
}

Configuration Fields

command: The executable to run (usually npx, node, or python).

args: Arguments passed to the command. For npx, this typically includes -y (auto-install) and the package name.

env: Environment variables passed to the server process. This is where credentials go.

Security Note

Never commit .mcp.json to git if it contains credentials. Add it to .gitignore. Instead, reference environment variables or use a separate credentials file.

exercises.question: Write a .mcp.json configuration that connects to an Obsidian MCP server using npx, with a vault path of ~/my-vault.
exercises.explanation: The configuration names the server 'obsidian', uses npx to auto-install the package, and passes the vault path as an environment variable.

exercises.question: Why should you NOT commit .mcp.json to git?
options: It may contain credentials like API keys and tokens
exercises.explanation: The env field in .mcp.json often contains sensitive credentials like API keys and service role keys. Committing these to git exposes them in the repository history.

exercises.question: What is the difference between project-level and global .mcp.json?
options: Global .mcp.json can only contain one server
Project-level servers are only available in that project; global servers are available everywhere
exercises.explanation: A .mcp.json in your project root only activates those servers when working in that project. ~/.mcp.json servers are available across all projects.

lessons.title: Using MCP Tools in Claude Code
lessons.description: Learn how MCP tools appear in Claude Code and how to use them effectively in your workflow.

sections.content:
Using MCP Tools

Once an MCP server is connected, its tools appear alongside Claude Code's built-in tools. Here's how to work with them.

Tool Discovery

MCP tools follow a naming convention: mcp__{server_name}__{tool_name}. For example:

  • mcp__supabase__query - Run a Supabase query
  • mcp__github__create_issue - Create a GitHub issue
  • mcp__obsidian__read_note - Read an Obsidian note

You don't need to know these names. Just ask Claude naturally:

"Query the users table in Supabase for all active accounts"

Claude will identify the right MCP tool and use it.

Permission System

MCP tools go through the same permission system as built-in tools. Claude will ask for approval before calling an MCP tool unless you've pre-approved it in your settings.

Tool Results

MCP tools return results just like built-in tools. Claude processes the results and continues the conversation. For example, a database query returns rows that Claude can analyze, summarize, or use to inform code changes.

Combining MCP with Built-in Tools

The real power is combining MCP tools with built-in tools:

  1. Use Supabase MCP to check the current database schema
  2. Use Grep to find all database queries in your code
  3. Use Edit to update queries to match a schema change
  4. Use Bash to run tests verifying the changes

This seamless combination of external services and local tools is what makes MCP powerful.

exercises.question: What is the naming convention for MCP tools in Claude Code?
exercises.explanation: MCP tools use double-underscore separators: mcp__{server_name}__{tool_name}. This namespacing prevents conflicts between tools from different servers.

exercises.question: Do you need to know MCP tool names to use them?
options: Yes, you must type the exact tool name
No, just ask Claude naturally and it will find the right tool
You need to list available tools with /tools first
Only for tools that require special permissions
exercises.explanation: Claude maps natural language requests to the correct MCP tool automatically. Ask 'query the users table' and Claude will invoke the appropriate Supabase MCP tool.

exercises.question: Put this MCP + built-in tool workflow in the correct order for updating code after a database schema change:
items: Use Edit to update queries in code
Use Supabase MCP to check the current schema
Use Grep to find all database queries in code
exercises.explanation: First check the schema (MCP), then find affected code (Grep), update the code (Edit), and verify with tests (Bash). This combines MCP and built-in tools in a logical sequence.

lessons.title: Building a Simple MCP Server
lessons.description: Build your own MCP server that exposes custom tools to Claude Code.

sections.content:
Building a Simple MCP Server

You can build custom MCP servers to expose internal tools, APIs, or data sources to Claude Code. Here's the basic structure.

Server Architecture

An MCP server is a program that:

  1. Communicates over stdin/stdout using the MCP protocol
  2. Declares available tools with their schemas
  3. Handles tool invocations and returns results

TypeScript MCP Server

import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { StdioServerTransport } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/stdio.js";
import { z } from "zod";

const server = new McpServer({
  name: "my-tools",
  version: "1.0.0"
});

// Define a tool
server.tool(
  "check-status",
  "Check the status of a service",
  { service: z.string().describe("The service name to check") },
  async ({ service }) => {
    // Your logic here
    const status = await checkService(service);
    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: JSON.stringify(status) }]
    };
  }
);

// Start the server
const transport = new StdioServerTransport();
await server.connect(transport);

Python MCP Server

from mcp.server import Server
from mcp.server.stdio import stdio_server

app = Server("my-tools")

@app.tool()
async def check_status(service: str) -> str:
    """Check the status of a service."""
    status = await check_service(service)
    return json.dumps(status)

async def main():
    async with stdio_server() as (read, write):
        await app.run(read, write)

Connecting Your Server

Add it to .mcp.json:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "my-tools": {
      "command": "node",
      "args": ["./mcp-server/index.js"]
    }
  }
}

exercises.question: How does an MCP server communicate with Claude Code?
exercises.explanation: MCP servers communicate over stdin/stdout using the standardized MCP protocol. This keeps the server simple and avoids network configuration.

exercises.question: What must an MCP server do when declaring a tool?
options: Provide the tool name, description, input schema, and handler function
Create a REST endpoint for each tool
exercises.explanation: Each tool needs a name (for invocation), description (so Claude knows when to use it), input schema (parameter types and validation), and a handler function (the actual implementation).

exercises.question: Write the .mcp.json entry to connect a Python MCP server located at ./tools/server.py.
exercises.explanation: For Python MCP servers, use python as the command and pass the script path as an argument. No env field is needed if the server doesn't require credentials.

lessons.title: Real-World MCP Integrations
lessons.description: Explore production MCP setups: Supabase, GitHub, Obsidian, and building compound workflows.

sections.content:
Real-World MCP Integrations

Let's look at how MCP servers are used in production workflows.

Supabase MCP

The Supabase MCP server gives Claude direct access to your database:

  • Query tables with SQL
  • Inspect schemas and relationships
  • Manage RLS policies
  • Run migrations

Workflow example: "Check if the users table has an email_verified column, and if not, add it with a migration that defaults existing users to verified."

GitHub MCP

The GitHub MCP server lets Claude manage your repository:

  • Create and manage issues
  • Review pull requests
  • Check CI/CD status
  • Manage releases

Workflow: "Create issues for each TODO comment in the codebase, labeled with the file path."

Obsidian MCP

The Obsidian MCP server connects Claude to your knowledge base:

  • Read and search notes
  • Create and update notes
  • Manage tags and frontmatter

Workflow: "Search my vault for all meeting notes about Project X and summarize the key decisions."

Compound Workflows

The real power comes from combining multiple MCP servers with built-in tools:

  1. GitHub MCP: Get the latest issue assigned to you
  2. Read the issue description and referenced files
  3. Supabase MCP: Check the current database schema
  4. Edit code to implement the fix
  5. Bash: Run tests
  6. GitHub MCP: Create a PR referencing the issue

This entire workflow can happen in a single Claude Code session with natural language instructions.

exercises.question: Which MCP server would you use to check if a database table has a specific column?
exercises.explanation: The Supabase MCP server provides database schema inspection tools. It can query table structures, columns, and relationships directly.

exercises.question: Write a .mcp.json that connects to both Supabase and GitHub MCP servers. Use environment variables for credentials.
exercises.explanation: Multiple MCP servers are configured as separate entries under mcpServers. Each has its own command, args, and environment variables. All servers start when Claude Code launches.

exercises.question: What makes compound MCP workflows powerful?
options: They combine multiple external services with built-in tools in a single session
They run faster than single-server workflows
exercises.explanation: Compound workflows chain together MCP tools (GitHub, Supabase) with built-in tools (Read, Edit, Bash) to accomplish complex multi-service tasks in a single conversational flow.

courses.title: Hooks & Automation
courses.description: Automate workflows with Claude Code hooks: pre/post tool execution, session lifecycle, and custom validation pipelines.

lessons.title: Pre/Post Tool Hooks
lessons.description: Run custom logic before and after Claude executes tools, enabling validation, logging, and guardrails.

sections.content:
Pre/Post Tool Hooks

Hooks let you run custom logic at specific points in Claude Code's execution. The most common are pre-tool and post-tool hooks that run before and after tool invocations.

What Hooks Can Do

  • Pre-tool: Validate inputs, enforce policies, log actions, block dangerous operations
  • Post-tool: Transform outputs, trigger notifications, update logs, run follow-up checks

Configuring Hooks

Hooks are configured in .claude/settings.json:

{
  "hooks": {
    "preTool": [
      {
        "tool": "bash",
        "command": "echo \"Running: $TOOL_INPUT\" >> .claude/tool-log.txt"
      }
    ],
    "postTool": [
      {
        "tool": "edit",
        "command": "npm run lint --fix $FILE_PATH"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Hook Environment Variables

Hooks receive context through environment variables:

  • $TOOL_NAME - The tool being invoked
  • $TOOL_INPUT - The tool's input parameters (JSON)
  • $FILE_PATH - The file being operated on (for file tools)
  • $TOOL_OUTPUT - The tool's output (post-tool hooks only)

Blocking Hooks

If a pre-tool hook exits with a non-zero status code, the tool invocation is blocked. This is how you enforce guardrails:

{
  "hooks": {
    "preTool": [
      {
        "tool": "bash",
        "command": "./scripts/check-destructive.sh $TOOL_INPUT"
      }
    ]
  }
}

The script can inspect the command and exit with code 1 to block destructive operations like rm -rf or git push --force.

exercises.question: How does a pre-tool hook block a tool from executing?
options: By deleting the tool's input file
By exiting with a non-zero status code
exercises.explanation: A pre-tool hook script that exits with a non-zero status code (e.g., exit 1) prevents the tool from executing. This is the standard Unix convention for indicating failure.

exercises.question: Which environment variable contains the tool's output in a post-tool hook?
exercises.explanation: $TOOL_OUTPUT contains the tool's return value and is only available in post-tool hooks, since the tool hasn't run yet during pre-tool hooks.

exercises.question: Write a post-tool hook configuration that runs 'prettier --write' on any file after the Edit tool modifies it.
exercises.explanation: This hook runs prettier on the edited file after every Edit tool invocation. $FILE_PATH is automatically set to the file that was modified.

lessons.title: Session Start Hooks
lessons.description: Run initialization logic when a Claude Code session begins, setting up the environment automatically.

sections.content:
Session Start Hooks

Session start hooks run when a new Claude Code session begins. They're perfect for environment setup, validation, and initialization tasks.

Configuration

{
  "hooks": {
    "sessionStart": [
      {
        "command": "./scripts/check-env.sh"
      },
      {
        "command": "npm run db:check"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Common Use Cases

Environment validation: Check that required environment variables are set before the session starts. Missing a database URL or API key? The hook can warn you immediately.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/check-env.sh
required_vars=("DATABASE_URL" "API_KEY" "NODE_ENV")
for var in "${required_vars[@]}"; do
  if [ -z "${!var}" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Missing required env var: $var"
    exit 1
  fi
done
echo "All required environment variables are set."

Service health checks: Verify that the database, Redis, or other services are running before Claude starts making changes that depend on them.

Dependency updates: Run npm install or pip install -r requirements.txt to ensure dependencies are current.

Git sync: Pull the latest changes from the remote before starting work.

Failure Behavior

If a session start hook fails (non-zero exit), Claude Code still starts but will show the error output. This ensures you're aware of issues without blocking your workflow entirely.

exercises.question: What happens if a session start hook fails?
options: Claude Code starts but shows the error output
Claude Code starts in read-only mode
exercises.explanation: Session start hooks don't block Claude Code from starting. If they fail, the error is shown so you're aware, but you can still use Claude Code. This prevents a broken hook from locking you out.

exercises.question: Write a session start hook configuration that runs a script to check environment variables and another to pull the latest git changes.
exercises.explanation: Multiple session start hooks run in order. First we validate the environment, then sync with the remote. This ensures every session starts with a clean, up-to-date state.

exercises.question: Which is NOT a good use case for session start hooks?
exercises.explanation: Running the full test suite at session start would slow down every session. Tests should be run when needed, not on every startup. Environment checks, service health checks, and git syncs are quick and appropriate.

lessons.title: Automated Linting and Validation
lessons.description: Set up hooks that automatically lint, format, and validate code after every edit.

sections.content:
Automated Linting and Validation

One of the most impactful uses of hooks is automatically running linters and formatters after Claude edits code. This ensures every change meets your project's standards without manual intervention.

Auto-Format on Edit

{
  "hooks": {
    "postTool": [
      {
        "tool": "edit",
        "command": "prettier --write $FILE_PATH 2>/dev/null || true"
      },
      {
        "tool": "write",
        "command": "prettier --write $FILE_PATH 2>/dev/null || true"
      }
    ]
  }
}

The || true prevents the hook from failing on non-JS files that prettier doesn't handle.

Type Checking After Edits

{
  "hooks": {
    "postTool": [
      {
        "tool": "edit",
        "command": "npx tsc --noEmit --pretty 2>&1 | head -20"
      }
    ]
  }
}

This runs TypeScript type checking after every edit. The head -20 limits output to the first 20 lines to avoid flooding the context.

Security Scanning

{
  "hooks": {
    "preTool": [
      {
        "tool": "bash",
        "command": "./scripts/block-secrets.sh $TOOL_INPUT"
      }
    ]
  }
}

A pre-tool hook on bash commands can scan for dangerous patterns (committing secrets, force-pushing, deleting databases) and block them before execution.

Combining Multiple Checks

You can chain multiple post-tool hooks. They run in order, and if any fails, subsequent hooks still execute (they're independent). This lets you run lint, format, and type-check as separate steps.

exercises.question: Why is '|| true' appended to the prettier hook command?
options: To make prettier run in verbose mode
To prevent the hook from failing on files prettier can't handle
To skip the git staging step
exercises.explanation: The || true ensures the hook always exits with code 0 (success). Without it, prettier failing on a non-JS file would cause the hook to report failure, even though the edit itself was fine.

exercises.question: Write a post-tool hook that runs ESLint with auto-fix on TypeScript files after any Edit operation, but only if the edited file is a .ts or .tsx file.
exercises.explanation: The bash conditional checks if the file extension is .ts or .tsx before running ESLint. The || true at the end prevents failure when the file isn't TypeScript or ESLint finds unfixable issues.

exercises.question: Why limit output with 'head -20' in a type-checking hook?
options: It makes the type checker run faster
To avoid flooding the context window with error output
The hook system has a 20-line output limit
exercises.explanation: Long error output from type checking consumes context window tokens. Limiting to 20 lines gives Claude enough information to identify the issue without wasting context space on repetitive errors.

lessons.title: Building Custom Workflows with Hooks
lessons.description: Combine multiple hooks into powerful automated workflows: CI pipelines, notification systems, and audit trails.

sections.content:
Building Custom Workflows

Hooks become powerful when combined into workflows. Here are patterns for building automated pipelines within Claude Code.

Commit Validation Pipeline

{
  "hooks": {
    "preTool": [
      {
        "tool": "bash",
        "command": "./scripts/pre-commit-check.sh $TOOL_INPUT"
      }
    ]
  }
}

The script detects git commit commands and runs checks:

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/pre-commit-check.sh
if echo "$1" | grep -q 'git commit'; then
  # Run tests
  npm test --silent || { echo "Tests failed. Commit blocked."; exit 1; }
  # Check for secrets
