  grep -rn 'sk-ant-\|ghp_\|password=' src/ && { echo "Secrets found!"; exit 1; }
fi

Notification Workflow

Post-tool hooks can send notifications when specific actions complete:

{
  "hooks": {
    "postTool": [
      {
        "tool": "bash",
        "command": "./scripts/notify-on-deploy.sh $TOOL_INPUT $TOOL_OUTPUT"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Audit Trail

Log every tool invocation with timestamps for compliance or debugging:

{
  "hooks": {
    "preTool": [
      {
        "command": "echo \"$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) $TOOL_NAME $TOOL_INPUT\" >> .claude/audit.log"
      }
    ]
  }
}

Note: This hook has no tool field, so it runs for ALL tool invocations.

Combined Workflow Example

A production-grade setup might combine: session start (env check + git pull), pre-tool (security scan + audit log), and post-tool (lint + format + type check). Each layer adds safety without slowing down the developer experience significantly.

exercises.question: What happens when a hook has no 'tool' field specified?
options: The hook only runs for bash commands
The hook runs for ALL tool invocations
exercises.explanation: Omitting the tool field makes the hook universal, it runs for every tool invocation. This is useful for audit logging and universal validation that should apply to all operations.

exercises.question: Write a pre-tool hook that blocks any bash command containing 'rm -rf' or 'drop table'.
exercises.explanation: The hook uses grep to check if the bash command contains destructive patterns. If found, it exits with code 1 to block execution. The || true at the end ensures non-matching commands pass through.

exercises.question: What is an audit trail hook useful for?
options: Recording every tool invocation for compliance and debugging
exercises.explanation: An audit trail hook logs every tool invocation with timestamps, creating a record of all actions Claude took. This is valuable for compliance requirements and debugging unexpected behavior.

lessons.title: The Hook Permission System
lessons.description: Understand how hooks interact with the permission system and security considerations for hook scripts.

sections.content:
The Hook Permission System

Hooks run outside of Claude's tool permission system. This gives them power but also requires careful security consideration.

How Hook Permissions Work

Hooks run as your user on your machine. They have the same permissions as any script you run in your terminal. This means:

  • They can read and write any file you have access to
  • They can execute any command available in your PATH
  • They can access environment variables, including secrets
  • They run BEFORE Claude's tool permission check (for pre-tool hooks)

Security Implications

Hooks bypass tool permissions: A post-tool hook can run commands that Claude would normally need permission for. This is by design (hooks are your code, not Claude's) but means you should audit hook scripts carefully.

Project hooks are a trust boundary: If you clone a project with hooks in .claude/settings.json, those hooks will run on your machine. Review them before trusting a new project's configuration.

Best Practices

  • Keep hook scripts simple and single-purpose
  • Don't put secrets directly in hook commands; use environment variables
  • Log hook outputs for debugging but be careful about logging sensitive data
  • Review hook configurations in projects you clone from others
  • Use || true in non-critical hooks to prevent cascading failures

Debugging Hooks

If a hook isn't working as expected:

  1. Run the hook command manually in your terminal to verify it works
  2. Check the hook's exit code (use echo $?)
  3. Add set -x at the start of hook scripts for verbose output
  4. Check that file paths and environment variables are resolved correctly

exercises.question: Why is it important to review hooks in a project you clone from someone else?
options: Hooks consume too much disk space
Hooks might conflict with your IDE
Hooks may not be compatible with your OS
Hooks run with your user permissions and could execute arbitrary commands
exercises.explanation: Hooks run as your user and can execute any command. A malicious hook could access your files, credentials, or network. Always review hook configurations in projects from untrusted sources.

exercises.question: Do hooks go through Claude's tool permission system?
options: Yes, they require the same approval as tools
No, hooks bypass the tool permission system entirely
Only hooks from other projects require approval
exercises.explanation: Hooks run outside Claude's permission system. They execute directly as your user, which gives them power but also means they need careful security review.

exercises.question: How do you debug a hook that isn't working?
options: Run the hook command manually in your terminal and check the exit code
exercises.explanation: The best debugging approach is to run the hook command manually, check its exit code with echo $?, and use set -x for verbose output. This isolates whether the issue is the command itself or how it's being invoked by Claude Code.

courses.title: Multi-Agent & Advanced Patterns
courses.description: Master Claude Code's multi-agent orchestration, parallel execution, git worktrees, and context management for complex workflows.

lessons.title: How Multi-Agent Orchestration Works
lessons.description: Understand the coordinator/sub-agent architecture that powers Claude Code's ability to handle complex, parallel tasks.

sections.content:
Multi-Agent Orchestration

Claude Code's most powerful capability is its multi-agent system. A coordinator agent can spawn and manage parallel sub-agents, each with isolated context, to tackle complex tasks simultaneously.

Architecture

The system has two layers:

Coordinator: The main Claude Code session you interact with. It understands the full task, breaks it down, and delegates to sub-agents.

Sub-agents: Spawned by the coordinator using the Agent tool. Each sub-agent gets its own context window, its own tool access, and works independently. Results flow back to the coordinator.

How It Works

  1. You give Claude a complex task
  2. The coordinator analyzes the task and identifies independent sub-tasks
  3. Sub-agents are spawned with specific instructions and context
  4. Sub-agents work in parallel, each reading/editing different files
  5. Results return to the coordinator, which synthesizes and continues

Key Properties

Isolated context: Each sub-agent has its own context window. It doesn't see other sub-agents' conversations or the full coordinator history. The coordinator passes only the relevant context.

Shared filesystem: All agents operate on the same filesystem. A file edited by one sub-agent is visible to others (though this can cause conflicts if not managed).

Independent tool access: Sub-agents can use all the same tools as the coordinator (Read, Edit, Bash, etc.), subject to the same permission settings.

exercises.question: What is the relationship between the coordinator and sub-agents?
options: The coordinator spawns sub-agents with specific tasks and isolated context
They communicate through shared memory files
They share the same context window
Sub-agents can spawn their own coordinators
exercises.explanation: The coordinator breaks down tasks and spawns sub-agents with specific instructions. Each sub-agent gets its own isolated context window and works independently.

exercises.question: What do sub-agents share with each other?
exercises.explanation: Sub-agents share the filesystem, they can all read and write the same files. However, their context windows and conversation histories are completely isolated from each other.

exercises.question: What is the potential risk of multiple sub-agents working on the same codebase?
options: They might use too much CPU
The API key might be rate-limited
Sub-agents can't read files written by other agents
Two agents might edit the same file, causing conflicts
exercises.explanation: Since sub-agents share the filesystem, two agents editing the same file simultaneously can cause conflicts. Good orchestration assigns non-overlapping file responsibilities to each agent.

lessons.title: Using the Agent Tool Effectively
lessons.description: Learn when and how to use the Agent tool for delegating tasks to sub-agents.

sections.content:
The Agent Tool

The Agent tool is how Claude Code spawns sub-agents. Understanding when to use it (and when not to) is key to effective multi-agent workflows.

When to Use Sub-Agents

Good candidates:

  • Independent tasks that don't depend on each other's output
  • Tasks that touch different parts of the codebase
  • Research tasks where multiple areas need investigation
  • Parallel test/fix cycles for different modules

Bad candidates:

  • Sequential tasks where step 2 depends on step 1's output
  • Tasks that all modify the same file
  • Simple tasks that are faster to do directly

Prompting Sub-Agents

When the coordinator spawns a sub-agent, the prompt it provides is critical. Good sub-agent prompts include:

  • The specific task to accomplish
  • Which files to work with (to avoid conflicts)
  • What constitutes success
  • Any constraints (don't modify X, use Y library)

The /batch Pattern

You can explicitly request parallel agent work:

"I need three things done in parallel:
1. Add input validation to the user API endpoints
2. Write unit tests for the payment module
3. Update the README with the new API documentation"

Claude recognizes these as independent tasks and can spawn sub-agents for each one.

exercises.question: Which set of tasks is the BEST candidate for parallel sub-agents?
options: Write tests for auth module, add validation to payments module, update API docs
Fix a bug, then verify the fix, then deploy
Read a config file, then modify it, then restart the server
Add a database column, then update the API to use it, then update the frontend
exercises.explanation: Tests for auth, validation for payments, and API docs are independent tasks touching different files. The other options are sequential, each step depends on the previous one.

exercises.question: What should a good sub-agent prompt include?
options: A list of all available tools
The specific task, which files to work with, success criteria, and constraints
exercises.explanation: Good sub-agent prompts are specific: what to do, where to do it, what success looks like, and what to avoid. This prevents conflicts and ensures focused, effective work.

exercises.question: Why should you avoid using sub-agents for tasks that modify the same file?
options: Sub-agents are slower than direct editing
The file system locks files during editing
Concurrent edits to the same file can cause conflicts
exercises.explanation: Two sub-agents editing the same file simultaneously can overwrite each other's changes. Assign non-overlapping file responsibilities to avoid conflicts.

lessons.title: Parallel vs Sequential Agent Execution
lessons.description: Learn when to run agents in parallel for speed vs sequentially for correctness, and how to manage dependencies.

sections.content:
Parallel vs Sequential Execution

Choosing between parallel and sequential agent execution is a critical decision that affects both speed and correctness.

Parallel Execution

Multiple sub-agents work simultaneously on independent tasks. This is faster but requires careful task decomposition to avoid conflicts.

Pattern:

"Run these in parallel:
- Agent A: Write unit tests for src/auth/
- Agent B: Write unit tests for src/payments/
- Agent C: Write unit tests for src/notifications/"

Each agent works on a different module. No shared files, no dependencies.

Sequential Execution

Tasks run one after another, each building on the previous result. Slower but necessary when there are dependencies.

Pattern:

"Do these in order:
1. First, design the database schema for user profiles
2. Then, implement the API endpoints using that schema
3. Finally, build the frontend components that call those endpoints"

Hybrid Approach

Often the best strategy combines both. Identify which tasks are independent (parallel) and which have dependencies (sequential):

Phase 1 (parallel): Design schema + write API specs
Phase 2 (sequential): Implement API based on Phase 1
Phase 3 (parallel): Write tests + update docs + build frontend

Dependency Detection

Claude naturally identifies dependencies when you describe the full scope. Mentioning "based on" or "using the output from" signals sequential requirements.

exercises.question: When should you use sequential execution instead of parallel?
options: When later tasks depend on the output of earlier tasks
When working on a small codebase
exercises.explanation: Sequential execution is necessary when tasks have dependencies. Building API endpoints requires the database schema to exist first; building the frontend requires the API to be defined.

exercises.question: Organize this hybrid workflow into the correct phases (parallel within each phase, sequential between phases):
items: Write unit tests for new endpoints
exercises.explanation: Schema must come first (dependency for API). API implementation second (depends on schema). Tests and docs can be parallel (both depend on API but not each other).

exercises.question: What language signals to Claude that tasks have dependencies?
options: Using phrases like 'based on', 'using the output from', 'after'
exercises.explanation: Dependency language like 'based on', 'using the output from', and 'after' signals to Claude that tasks must be sequential. Claude uses these cues to plan execution order.

lessons.title: Git Worktrees for Agent Isolation
lessons.description: Use git worktrees to give each agent its own working copy, eliminating file conflicts in parallel workflows.

sections.content:
Git Worktrees for Agent Isolation

The biggest challenge with parallel agents is file conflicts. Git worktrees solve this by giving each agent its own working copy of the repository.

What Are Git Worktrees?

A git worktree is an additional working directory linked to the same repository. Each worktree has its own branch and file state, but they share the same git history and objects.

git worktree add ../my-project-feature-auth feature/auth
git worktree add ../my-project-feature-payments feature/payments

Now you have three directories, all linked to the same repo:

  • my-project/ (main worktree)
  • my-project-feature-auth/ (auth feature)
  • my-project-feature-payments/ (payments feature)

Worktrees + Multi-Agent

Each sub-agent can work in its own worktree:

  1. Coordinator creates worktrees for each parallel task
  2. Agent A works in worktree-auth on authentication changes
  3. Agent B works in worktree-payments on payment changes
  4. When both finish, coordinator merges the branches

No file conflicts, because each agent has its own copy of the files.

Claude Code Integration

Claude Code has built-in worktree support. The EnterWorktree and ExitWorktree tools manage worktree creation and cleanup. You can ask:

"Create a worktree for the auth feature and implement JWT token validation there"

Cleanup

After merging, remove worktrees to keep your workspace clean:

git worktree remove ../my-project-feature-auth

exercises.question: What problem do git worktrees solve in multi-agent workflows?
options: File conflicts when multiple agents edit the same codebase simultaneously
exercises.explanation: Worktrees give each agent its own working directory with its own file state. Agents can freely edit any file without conflicting with other agents' changes.

exercises.question: What do git worktrees share with each other?
options: The same git history and objects, but separate file states
exercises.explanation: Worktrees share the same git repository (history, objects, refs) but each has its own checked-out branch and file state. This makes them lightweight to create.

exercises.question: Write the git commands to create two worktrees for parallel feature development: one for 'feature/search' and one for 'feature/notifications'.
exercises.correct_answer:
git worktree add ../project-search feature/search
git worktree add ../project-notifications feature/notifications
exercises.explanation: Each git worktree add command creates a new directory with a checkout of the specified branch. If the branch doesn't exist, git creates it automatically.

lessons.title: Context Management Across Agents
lessons.description: Strategies for managing context windows, sharing information between agents, and keeping sessions efficient.

sections.content:
Context Management Across Agents

Each agent has its own context window. Managing what information goes where is crucial for effective multi-agent workflows.

Context Isolation

Sub-agents start with a clean context. They don't inherit the coordinator's full conversation. The coordinator provides only the relevant context when spawning a sub-agent. This is a feature, not a bug; focused context means better results.

What to Pass to Sub-Agents

  • Always: The specific task description and success criteria
  • Usually: Relevant file paths, coding conventions, constraints
  • Sometimes: Previous decisions that affect the sub-agent's work
  • Never: Unrelated conversation history, other agents' tasks

Sharing Results Between Agents

When Agent A's output is needed by Agent B, the coordinator manages this:

  1. Agent A completes its task and returns results
  2. Coordinator extracts the relevant output
  3. Coordinator passes that context to Agent B when spawning it

This is why sequential execution is needed for dependent tasks. The coordinator acts as the information bridge.

Context Window Strategies

Keep sessions focused: One feature or bug per session. Context from unrelated work wastes tokens.

Use CLAUDE.md as shared state: Information in CLAUDE.md is loaded for every agent. Put architectural decisions and conventions there so sub-agents know the rules.

File-based communication: For complex data handoffs between agents, write results to a file. The next agent can read the file to get the full context without the coordinator having to pass it all in the prompt.

Summary files: After major changes, write a summary to a known location (e.g., .claude/session-notes.md). Other agents can read this for context about what's happened.

exercises.question: Why do sub-agents NOT inherit the coordinator's full conversation history?
options: The API doesn't support context sharing
Focused context leads to better results; irrelevant history wastes tokens
It would be too slow to transfer
exercises.explanation: Context isolation is intentional. A sub-agent tasked with writing tests doesn't need the conversation about deployment strategy. Focused context means more room for the actual work and better-quality output.

exercises.question: How does the coordinator share Agent A's results with Agent B when they have dependencies?
options: Agent B reads Agent A's context window
The coordinator extracts relevant output from A and passes it to B when spawning it
Agents communicate directly with each other
exercises.explanation: The coordinator acts as an information bridge. It receives Agent A's results, extracts what's relevant, and includes it in the prompt when spawning Agent B.

exercises.question: What is the advantage of using files for complex data handoffs between agents?
options: Files are faster to access than prompts
The full data persists on disk rather than needing to fit in the coordinator's prompt
Claude can only read files, not prompts
exercises.explanation: For large or complex data (like a database schema or API specification), writing to a file lets the next agent read the full content directly. Passing it through the coordinator's prompt would consume context window space.

courses.title: Real Project: Build a Full App
courses.description: Apply everything you've learned by building a complete application with Claude Code, from project setup through production deployment.

lessons.title: Case Study: Building a Voice Agent with Claude Code
lessons.description: Walk through a real-world case study of building a production voice agent application using Claude Code.

sections.content:
Case Study: Voice Agent Application

In this course, we'll walk through building a complete application using Claude Code. The example is a voice agent, a server that handles incoming phone calls with AI-powered conversation. This mirrors a real production system.

The Application

We're building a FastAPI webhook server that:

  • Receives webhooks from a voice AI platform (VAPI)
  • Processes call events (call started, speech detected, call ended)
  • Manages conversation state with an LLM
  • Logs call data to a Supabase database
  • Sends notifications on important events

Tech Stack

  • Runtime: Python 3.12
  • Framework: FastAPI
  • Database: Supabase (PostgreSQL)
  • Voice: VAPI for call handling
  • LLM: Groq for fast inference
  • Deployment: Render

Why This Case Study?

This project exercises every Claude Code skill we've covered:

  • CLAUDE.md and project configuration (Course 2)
  • Plan mode for complex features (Course 3)
  • Custom skills for deployment workflows (Course 4)
  • MCP for database management (Course 5)
  • Hooks for validation (Course 6)
  • Multi-agent for parallel feature development (Course 7)

Over the next four lessons, we'll build this step by step, exactly as you would with Claude Code in a real workflow.

exercises.question: Why is a voice agent application a good case study for Claude Code skills?
options: Voice agents are simple to build
It only requires one Claude Code session
It exercises all major Claude Code features: config, planning, skills, MCP, hooks, and multi-agent
Voice agents are the most popular type of application
exercises.explanation: A voice agent touches database management (MCP), multi-file changes (plan mode), deployment workflows (skills/hooks), and parallel development (multi-agent). It naturally exercises the full Claude Code skill set.

exercises.question: What is the role of the FastAPI server in this architecture?
options: It makes phone calls to users
It receives webhooks from the voice platform and processes call events
It runs the AI model locally
exercises.explanation: The FastAPI server acts as a webhook receiver. VAPI sends events (call started, speech detected, call ended) to the server, which processes them and manages conversation state.

exercises.question: Which database is used in this case study?
exercises.explanation: The project uses Supabase, which provides a managed PostgreSQL database with additional features like Row Level Security and real-time subscriptions.

lessons.title: Project Setup: CLAUDE.md, Rules, Memory
lessons.description: Set up the project with proper CLAUDE.md configuration, rule files, and initial memory structure.

sections.content:
Project Setup

Before writing any application code, we set up Claude Code's project configuration. This upfront investment pays dividends throughout development.

Step 1: CLAUDE.md

# Voice Agent Webhook Server

FastAPI webhook server for VAPI voice agents.

## Tech Stack
- Runtime: Python 3.12
- Framework: FastAPI + uvicorn
- Database: Supabase (PostgreSQL via supabase-py)
- Voice: VAPI webhooks
- LLM: Groq (llama-3.3-70b)
- Deployment: Render (auto-deploys from main)

## Key Commands
- `uvicorn webhook_server:app --reload` - Start dev server
- `pytest` - Run tests
- `ruff check .` - Lint
- `ruff format .` - Format

## Project Structure
webhook_server.py   # Main FastAPI app, webhook handlers
config.py           # Environment variables and constants
db.py               # Supabase client and queries
tests/              # pytest test files

## Conventions
- All env vars loaded via os.environ[] (raise on missing)
- Parameterized queries only (no string concatenation)
- Every new Supabase table must have RLS policies
- Webhook endpoints must validate payload signatures

Step 2: Security Rules

Create .claude/rules/security.md:

---
globs: ["**/*.py"]
---

# Security Rules
- Never hardcode secrets in source code
- All user inputs must be validated
- SQL injection prevention: parameterized queries only
- Sanitize data before logging (no PII in logs)
- Validate webhook signatures on all endpoints

Step 3: Initial Memory

Create .claude/memory/MEMORY.md with initial project notes. This will grow as you develop.

Step 4: Settings

Create .claude/settings.json with allowed tools and hooks for auto-formatting.

exercises.question: Write the Key Commands section of CLAUDE.md for a Node.js Express project with TypeScript, using Jest for tests and ESLint for linting.
exercises.correct_answer:
## Key Commands
- `npm run dev` - Start dev server with hot reload
- `npm test` - Run Jest test suite
- `npm run lint` - Run ESLint
- `npm run build` - Compile TypeScript to JavaScript
- `npm start` - Start production server
exercises.explanation: Key Commands should cover the full development lifecycle: dev server, testing, linting, building, and production start. This gives Claude everything it needs to develop, test, and deploy.

exercises.question: Write a .claude/rules/api.md file with rules for API endpoint development, targeting all TypeScript files in the routes/ directory.
exercises.correct_answer:
---
globs: ["src/routes/**/*.ts"]
---

# API Rules

- Validate all request inputs using zod schemas
- Return appropriate HTTP status codes (400 for bad input, 401 for unauthorized, 404 for not found, 500 for server errors)
- Include rate limiting middleware on public endpoints
- Never expose internal error details to clients
- All endpoints must have request/response type definitions
- Log errors with context but without PII
exercises.explanation: The glob targets only route files. Rules are specific and actionable: validation library (zod), status codes to use, rate limiting requirement, and error handling guidelines.

exercises.question: Why set up CLAUDE.md and rules BEFORE writing application code?
options: It speeds up the API response time
It ensures every line of code Claude writes follows your project's conventions from the start
Claude Code won't work without CLAUDE.md
It's required for the API to accept requests
exercises.explanation: Setting up configuration first means Claude follows your conventions from the very first line of code. Retrofitting conventions after code is written requires refactoring, which is slower and error-prone.

exercises.question: What should a project's .claude/settings.json contain for development efficiency?
options: Allowed tools for common commands and hooks for auto-formatting
exercises.explanation: Settings.json contains tool permissions (pre-approve safe commands like tests and lint) and hooks (auto-format after edits). This reduces permission prompts and ensures consistent code quality.

lessons.title: Feature Implementation: Iterating with Plan Mode
lessons.description: Build core features using plan mode for complex changes and direct execution for quick iterations.

sections.content:
Feature Implementation

With the project configured, we implement features using the plan mode patterns from Course 3. Here's how a real feature development cycle looks.

Feature: Webhook Handler

Prompt to Claude:

"Plan first. I need a webhook endpoint at /webhook that:
- Accepts POST requests with JSON payload
- Validates the VAPI webhook signature
- Handles three event types: call.started, call.speech, call.ended
- Logs call data to Supabase
- Returns appropriate responses to VAPI

Show me the plan before implementing."

Claude's Plan

Claude produces a structured plan:

  1. Create the webhook route in webhook_server.py
  2. Add signature validation helper in config.py
  3. Create event handler functions for each event type
  4. Add Supabase insert functions in db.py
  5. Write tests for each handler

You review the plan, suggest adjustments ("also add error handling for malformed payloads"), and approve.

Iterative Development

After the initial implementation, iterate with direct execution:

"The call.ended handler should also calculate call duration and store it"
"Add a retry mechanism for Supabase writes that fail"
"The signature validation is rejecting valid requests - fix it"

These are small, focused changes. No plan needed, Claude executes directly.

The Edit-Test Loop

The most productive pattern: make a change, run tests, fix failures, repeat. Ask Claude to:

"Run the tests after each change and fix any failures before moving on"

Claude enters an autonomous loop: edit, test, fix, test, until everything passes.

exercises.question: Write a prompt for Claude Code that implements a new feature (user authentication with JWT) using the plan-first pattern. Include what you want in the plan.
exercises.correct_answer:
Plan first. I need to add JWT-based authentication to the API:
- Users should be able to register with email/password
- Login endpoint returns a JWT token
- Protected endpoints verify the JWT token
- Tokens expire after 24 hours
- Passwords must be hashed with bcrypt

In your plan, show me: which files will be created or modified, the order of implementation, and any new dependencies needed. Don't write code until I approve the plan.
exercises.explanation: This prompt explicitly requests plan mode, describes the feature with specific requirements, and tells Claude what the plan should include. The 'don't write code until I approve' ensures you review first.

exercises.question: What is the 'edit-test loop' pattern?
options: A git workflow for feature branches
A manual process of editing and testing
Claude autonomously edits code, runs tests, fixes failures, and repeats until all tests pass
exercises.explanation: The edit-test loop is Claude Code's most productive pattern. Claude makes changes, runs the test suite, identifies failures, fixes them, and re-tests automatically until everything passes.

exercises.question: When should you use plan mode vs direct execution during feature development?
options: Plan mode for initial complex implementation; direct execution for small iterations
Plan mode for bug fixes; direct execution for new features
exercises.explanation: Plan mode shines for the initial complex implementation where you want to review the approach. Once the structure is in place, small iterations (fix this, add that) are faster with direct execution.

lessons.title: Testing and Debugging with Claude Code
lessons.description: Use Claude Code's debugging workflows: systematic diagnosis, test-driven fixes, and error interpretation.

sections.content:
Testing and Debugging

Claude Code excels at debugging because it can read code, run tests, analyze errors, and fix issues in a single workflow loop.

Test-Driven Development

Ask Claude to write tests first:

"Write tests for the webhook signature validation. Cover:
- Valid signature passes
- Invalid signature is rejected
- Missing signature header returns 401
- Expired timestamp is rejected"

Then implement until the tests pass. This gives Claude a clear definition of "done."

Debugging Workflow

When you hit a bug, give Claude the symptoms:

"The /webhook endpoint returns 500 when VAPI sends a call.ended event.
Here's the error from the logs: [paste error]
Fix it."

Claude's debugging process:

  1. Read the error message and stack trace
  2. Read the relevant source files
  3. Identify the root cause
  4. Propose and implement a fix
  5. Run tests to verify the fix
  6. Check for similar issues elsewhere in the codebase

Interpreting Error Messages

Claude is exceptionally good at interpreting cryptic error messages. Stack traces, database errors, authentication failures, and deployment logs are all fair game. Just paste the error and ask for help.

Writing Better Tests

Ask Claude to improve test coverage:

"Look at the current test coverage for db.py and add tests for any untested edge cases, especially error paths."

Claude will read the source, read the tests, identify gaps, and write additional test cases.

exercises.question: Put Claude's debugging workflow in the correct order:
items: Read the error message and stack trace
Run tests to verify the fix
exercises.explanation: Claude reads the error, examines source code, finds the root cause, implements a fix, verifies with tests, and then proactively checks for similar issues. This systematic approach prevents leaving related bugs behind.

exercises.question: Why is giving Claude the error symptoms better than telling it where to look?
options: The root cause may be in a different file than where you expect
Claude can't navigate to specific files
exercises.explanation: Bugs often manifest in one place but originate in another. Providing symptoms lets Claude trace the root cause wherever it is, rather than looking where you assume the problem is.

exercises.question: Write a prompt that asks Claude to write tests first (TDD style) for a function that validates email addresses, then implement the function.
exercises.correct_answer:
Write tests first for an email validation function in src/utils/validators.ts. Test cases should cover:
- Valid emails pass (standard format, subdomains, plus addressing)
- Invalid emails fail (no @, no domain, double dots, spaces)
- Edge cases (very long local parts, unicode characters)

Then implement the validateEmail function to make all tests pass. Run tests after implementation to verify.
exercises.explanation: TDD prompts specify the test cases before asking for implementation. This gives Claude a concrete definition of success and ensures comprehensive coverage from the start.

lessons.title: Deploying to Production
lessons.description: Use Claude Code's full toolkit to prepare, deploy, and verify a production release.

sections.content:
Deploying to Production

The final step: getting your application live. This lesson combines everything: skills for deployment workflow, hooks for validation, and MCP for database management.

Pre-Deployment Checklist

Use the deploy-ready skill from Course 4 (or ask Claude directly):

"Run a pre-deployment check: tests, lint, security scan, build verification, and git status."

Database Migration

If your feature requires database changes, use the Supabase MCP:

"Check the current Supabase schema and run the migration in db/migrations/002_add_call_duration.sql"

Claude uses the Supabase MCP to inspect the current schema, verify the migration is safe, and execute it.

Environment Variables

Verify all required environment variables are configured on the deployment platform:

"Check that all env vars used in config.py are set in the Render environment. List any missing ones."

The Deployment

For git-triggered deployments (Render, Vercel), the deploy is a git push:

"Create a commit with all changes, push to main, and monitor the deployment."

Post-Deployment Verification

After deployment, verify the application is working:

"Hit the /health endpoint on the production URL and verify it returns 200. Then send a test webhook payload to /webhook and check the logs."

Rollback Plan

Always have a rollback ready. Claude can help:

"If anything goes wrong, what's the git revert command to roll back this deployment?"

Claude will identify the commit to revert and give you the exact command.

Congratulations

You've completed the full journey from installation to production deployment. You now have the skills to use Claude Code as a true development partner, not just a code assistant.

exercises.question: Put the production deployment steps in the correct order:
exercises.explanation: Pre-deployment checks first, then database migration (so the schema is ready), verify env vars, prepare rollback, push to deploy, and finally verify the deployment is working.

exercises.question: Why should you run database migrations BEFORE deploying the new code?
options: The database is locked during deployment
The new code expects the updated schema; deploying first could cause errors
Migrations can only run on the old code
exercises.explanation: If the new code references columns or tables that don't exist yet, it will fail. Running the migration first ensures the database schema matches what the code expects.

exercises.question: Write a prompt for Claude Code that performs a complete post-deployment verification for a FastAPI application deployed on Render.
exercises.correct_answer:
Verify the production deployment at https://my-app.onrender.com:
1. Check the /health endpoint returns 200 with a valid response body
2. Send a test POST to /webhook with a sample call.started payload and verify it returns 200
3. Check the Render deployment logs for any errors in the last 5 minutes
4. Query Supabase to confirm the test call was logged correctly
5. Report pass/fail for each check
exercises.explanation: Post-deployment verification should test the health endpoint, exercise the main functionality, check logs for errors, and verify data persistence. Each check should have a clear pass/fail criteria.
